ir-hunts.title: Kerberoastable service accounts hold Domain Admin membership, enabling one-step full domain compromise
ir-hunts.premise: Any Active Directory account with a registered Service Principal Name (SPN) can have its Kerberos TGS ticket requested by any authenticated domain user; no special privilege required. If that account also holds Domain Admins membership, a single offline RC4-HMAC ticket crack yields full domain compromise. This misconfiguration is trivially enumerable by any attacker with a foothold and is a common first pivot in AD-based intrusions. Quarterly AD privilege review model flagged SPN accounts in privileged groups for validation.
ir-hunts.huntScope: All Active Directory user accounts across corp.local with a servicePrincipalName attribute set. Cross-reference against Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, and Backup Operators group membership. No special audit policy required; LDAP read access to AD is sufficient.
ir-hunts.dataSourcesNeeded:
Active Directory LDAP query; PowerShell Get-ADUser with Filter {ServicePrincipalName -ne "$null"} plus group membership lookup
Kerberos audit logs (Event ID 4769); identify RC4-HMAC etype 0x17 TGS requests against discovered SPNs (retroactive baselining)
AD ACL audit; confirm no additional permissions delegated beyond group membership
ir-hunts.dataSourcesAvailable:
AD PowerShell module (available on any domain-joined host with RSAT)
Microsoft Sentinel; Security Event connector forwarding DC logs
MDE Advanced Hunting; IdentityQueryEvents table
ir-hunts.conclusionStatement:
CONFIRMED. Two Kerberoastable Domain Admin accounts identified:

1. svc-portal-wubba@corp.local; SPN: HTTP/portal-wubba.corp.local; Domain Admins member; no AES enforcement (RC4-HMAC permitted); password last set 847 days ago.

2. svc-sqlreport@corp.local; SPN: MSSQLSvc/sql01.corp.local:1433; Domain Admins member; no AES enforcement; password last set 412 days ago.

Both accounts are trivially Kerberoastable. Offline cracking of a typical 8–12 character service account password on a GPU cluster takes minutes to hours. Full domain compromise is achievable by any authenticated domain user without generating additional alerts.

Remediation dispatched to IAM team: (1) remove both accounts from Domain Admins immediately, (2) enforce AES-only Kerberos on all SPN accounts, (3) reset passwords to 25+ character random strings, (4) initiate quarterly SPN-to-privilege audit as a standing process.

techniques.name: Kerberoasting
techniques.expectedIocs:
Event ID 4769 on domain controllers with EncryptionType 0x17 (RC4-HMAC) for SPN accounts; especially from workstations or non-service hosts
Large bursts of 4769 requests from a single source IP in a short window (automated Kerberoasting tooling)
Impacket GetUserSPNs.py, Rubeus kerberoast, or Invoke-Kerberoast process activity on any host
techniques.notes: RC4-HMAC downgrade is the key signal; legitimate modern services negotiate AES256 (etype 0x12) or AES128 (etype 0x11). An etype 0x17 request for a sensitive SPN should be treated as an adversarial indicator unless you have a documented legacy system requiring RC4. AES enforcement via msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes is the primary mitigating control.
techniques.queryIdeas:
// Event 4769 RC4-HMAC TGS requests on DCs; Sentinel KQL
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4769
| where TicketEncryptionType == "0x17"  // RC4-HMAC
| where ServiceName !endswith "$"       // exclude machine accounts
| where ServiceName !in ("krbtgt", "kadmin")
| summarize count() by bin(TimeGenerated, 1h), SubjectUserName, ServiceName, IpAddress
| where count_ > 5
| sort by TimeGenerated desc

// PowerShell: find all Kerberoastable DA accounts
Get-ADUser -Filter {ServicePrincipalName -ne "$null"} -Properties ServicePrincipalName,MemberOf |
Where-Object { $_.MemberOf -match "Domain Admins|Enterprise Admins" } |
Select-Object SamAccountName, ServicePrincipalName, PasswordLastSet

techniques.name: Domain Accounts
techniques.expectedIocs:
Successful logon (Event 4624, Logon Type 3) from svc-portal-wubba or svc-sqlreport from non-expected source IPs
Any interactive logon (Logon Type 2 or 10) for a service account; service accounts should never log on interactively
techniques.notes: Once cracked, the plaintext password can be used in Pass-the-Password attacks or direct logon. Monitor these accounts for any authentication outside their documented service contexts. Set a Sentinel alert on logon type 2/10 for service accounts as a standing detection.
techniques.queryIdeas:
// Monitor service accounts for interactive or anomalous logon
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4624
| where TargetUserName in ("svc-portal-wubba", "svc-sqlreport")
| where LogonType in (2, 10)  // Interactive, RemoteInteractive
| project TimeGenerated, TargetUserName, LogonType, IpAddress, WorkstationName

ir-hunts.title: Conditional Access does not restrict the OAuth 2.0 device authorization grant flow: tenant-wide MFA bypass exposure for all standard users
ir-hunts.premise: The OAuth 2.0 device authorization grant (device code flow) allows a user to authenticate by entering a short code at the legitimate Microsoft devicelogin.microsoft.com portal. Because the victim authenticates at a real Microsoft URL (not a phishing page), URL-reputation controls, Safe Links, and browser-based phishing heuristics do not fire. The attacker polls the Microsoft /token endpoint directly; the token is issued to the attacker's IP, not the victim's. Most Conditional Access policies apply to interactive sign-in flows and do not evaluate or block the /devicecode request. If the tenant has no explicit CA policy targeting this grant type, any user with a valid M365 account can be silently compromised through a device code lure, bypassing MFA enforcement. Recent MSTIC and CISA advisories confirm this technique is actively exploited by multiple nation-state actors.
ir-hunts.huntScope: Microsoft Entra ID; all Conditional Access policies, sign-in logs (NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs and SigninLogs), and audit logs for the trailing 90 days. Focus: identify CA policy coverage for device code flow; retroactively check for device code authentication events from anomalous source IPs.
ir-hunts.dataSourcesNeeded:
Entra ID Conditional Access policy export; verify grant type coverage for all policies
NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs; device code flow events appear here, NOT in standard SigninLogs
SigninLogs filtered by ClientAppUsed == 'Device code' or AuthenticationProtocol == 'deviceCode'
Entra ID Audit Logs; ConsentGrant events following device code authentication from anomalous IPs
ir-hunts.dataSourcesAvailable:
Microsoft Sentinel; Entra ID diagnostic settings forwarding SigninLogs (confirmed)
NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs; NOT currently forwarded to Sentinel (log source gap discovered during this hunt)
Entra ID portal; CA policy export available to Global Reader
ir-hunts.conclusionStatement:
DATA GAP; cannot close hunt without remediation.

CA policy review: Confirmed; no Conditional Access policy in the tenant references the device code grant type. Existing MFA policies apply to interactive sign-in only (ClientAppUsed targeting browser and modern auth clients). The device authorization grant flow is entirely uncontrolled.

Log coverage: NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs are not forwarded to Sentinel. Standard SigninLogs do not reliably capture device code flow events. 90-day retroactive log pull via Entra ID portal for device code events returned 0 results; consistent with the log source gap, not evidence of absence.

Recommended immediate actions:
(1) Deploy CA policy: block device code flow for all users except a documented exceptions group (kiosk/shared device scenarios).
(2) Enable NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs ingestion to Sentinel (Entra ID diagnostic settings → add log category).
(3) Re-run retroactive hunt in 30 days once log source confirmed active.
(4) Hunt cannot be closed as denied; absence of evidence is not evidence of absence here.

techniques.name: Steal Application Access Token
techniques.expectedIocs:
Entra ID sign-in events with AuthenticationProtocol == 'deviceCode' from IPs inconsistent with user's known locations
Sign-in from a new country for an account with no travel history; within 1 hour of a device code lure email delivery
New OAuth app consent grant within 30 minutes of a successful device code authentication from an anomalous IP
techniques.notes: Device code flow authentication events appear in NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs, not SigninLogs. This is the key log source gap. Many tenants that forward SigninLogs to Sentinel believe they have full auth coverage but are blind to device code activity. This hunt discovered the gap before exploitation; the control weakness is confirmed regardless of whether past exploitation is detectable.
techniques.queryIdeas:
// Sentinel KQL; device code sign-in detection (requires NonInteractiveUserSignInLogs)
AADNonInteractiveUserSignInLogs
| where AuthenticationProtocol == "deviceCode" or ClientAppUsed == "Device code"
| where IPAddress !in (known_vpn_ips) // replace with your VPN/office egress
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location, AppDisplayName, ResultDescription
| sort by TimeGenerated desc

// Detect consent grants post device-code auth (correlation)
AuditLogs
| where OperationName == "Consent to application"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| extend InitiatedBy = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName)
| join kind=inner (
    AADNonInteractiveUserSignInLogs
    | where AuthenticationProtocol == "deviceCode"
    | project UserPrincipalName, DeviceCodeTime = TimeGenerated
) on $left.InitiatedBy == $right.UserPrincipalName
| where TimeGenerated between (DeviceCodeTime .. (DeviceCodeTime + 1h))

techniques.name: Application Access Token
techniques.expectedIocs:
App-only sign-ins from non-SNI IP ranges using tokens issued via device code flow
Refresh token issued with 90-day lifetime to non-corporate IP; detected by token binding absence
techniques.notes: Device code–issued refresh tokens have 90-day TTL and are bound to no device (no Intune device compliance, no PRT, no FIDO2). Once issued to an attacker IP, the token is refreshed silently from that IP indefinitely until revoked. Standard CA policies evaluating sign-in risk do not trigger on refresh token use; only initial issuance is evaluated. This means even after a CA policy is deployed, previously issued tokens remain valid.

ir-hunts.title: Vulnerable kernel drivers (LOLDrivers catalog: RTCore64, WinRing0x64, mhyprot2 variants) loaded or staged on domain-joined endpoints, enabling BYOVD EDR bypass
ir-hunts.premise: Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) attacks load a legitimately signed but exploitable kernel driver as a Windows service, then use its documented-vulnerable IOCTLs to write arbitrary kernel memory; including removing EDR callback registrations. The LOLDrivers project catalogs hundreds of such drivers. RTCore64.sys (CVE-2019-16098) is particularly prevalent: it accepts unauthenticated kernel memory read/write via IOCTL and has been used by multiple nation-state groups to blind CrowdStrike Falcon, Sophos, ESET, and others. A proactive hunt checking our endpoint fleet's loaded and staged drivers against the LOLDrivers SHA256 catalog would identify any pre-positioned capability.
ir-hunts.huntScope: All 847 CrowdStrike Falcon-enrolled endpoints and MDE-enrolled devices. Driver file inventory (C:\Windows\System32\drivers\, C:\Windows\SysWOW64\), Windows Service registry (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services), and Sysmon Event 6 (driver loaded) logs.
ir-hunts.dataSourcesNeeded:
MDE Advanced Hunting; DeviceFileCertificateInfo and DeviceFileEvents tables for driver SHA256 hash hunting
CrowdStrike Falcon; Discover module driver inventory query
Windows Event ID 7045 (new service installed) on all endpoints forwarded to Sentinel
Sysmon Event ID 6 (driver loaded); requires Sysmon deployed with a configuration capturing ImageLoaded events
ir-hunts.dataSourcesAvailable:
MDE Advanced Hunting; available (E5 license)
CrowdStrike Falcon Discover; available
Sentinel Windows Security Events connector; 7045 forwarded
Sysmon; deployed on 612 of 847 endpoints (72% coverage); 235 endpoints missing Sysmon
ir-hunts.conclusionStatement:
DENIED; no LOLDriver matches found.

Hash hunt scope: 847 MDE-enrolled endpoints cross-referenced against the top 50 high-risk LOLDriver SHA256 hashes (RTCore64.sys variants: 3 hashes; WinRing0x64.sys: 2 hashes; mhyprot2.sys: 4 hashes; gdrv.sys: 2 hashes; ProcExp152.sys: 1 hash; AsrDrv103.sys: 2 hashes; remaining 36 from LOLDrivers 'high severity' tier). Zero matches returned.

False-positive review: 14 drivers with revoked Authenticode certificates found; all confirmed as legitimate legacy RAID controller and NIC vendor drivers, each present in vendor allowlist with documented justification.

Hunt closed as denied. No evidence of pre-positioned BYOVD capability across the current fleet.

Standing recommendations: (1) Enable Microsoft HVCI (Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity); blocks unsigned and revoked drivers at hypervisor level, eliminates most BYOVD attack surface. (2) Deploy Microsoft WDAC driver blocklist including all LOLDrivers high-severity hashes. (3) Expand Sysmon coverage to remaining 235 endpoints for Event 6 (driver loaded) visibility.

techniques.name: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.expectedIocs:
Driver SHA256 matching LOLDrivers catalog on any host
Windows Event 7045 for kernel-mode driver with revoked or untrusted Authenticode certificate
Falcon or MDE sensor heartbeat gap >5 minutes outside documented maintenance window
Registry key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<suspicious_name> with ImagePath pointing to unusual driver location
techniques.notes: Event 7045 is available on all Windows hosts without Sysmon or advanced audit policy. Filtering to 'kernel mode driver' and correlating to certificate validity is the highest-fidelity signal available. The revoked certificate check is the key differentiator; legitimate vendor drivers are typically signed with valid EV certificates.
techniques.queryIdeas:
// MDE hash hunt against LOLDrivers catalog
let loldrivers = dynamic([
    "3a1c5c476e8c1c8f7be0ae17e5dacc85",  // RTCore64.sys v1.0.0.0
    "9b2e5d8a1c4f7e0d3b6a9c2f5e8b1d4a"   // add remaining hashes
]);
DeviceFileEvents
| where SHA256 in (loldrivers)
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, FolderPath, SHA256, InitiatingProcessFileName

// Sentinel: kernel driver installs with revoked certs (requires Sysmon Event 6)
Event
| where EventID == 6  // Sysmon driver loaded
| extend imageLoaded = tostring(parse_xml(EventData).DataItem.EventData.Data[0])
| where imageLoaded contains "Signed: false" or imageLoaded contains "Revoked: true"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, imageLoaded

techniques.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
techniques.expectedIocs:
IOCTL calls to vulnerable driver from unexpected process (not the driver's associated service)
Kernel-level write operations from user-mode process after driver load
EDR heartbeat loss coinciding with new kernel driver load on same host
techniques.notes: The IOCTL exploitation phase happens entirely in kernel memory and produces no file artifacts; detection depends on catching the driver load (Event 7045/Sysmon 6) before exploitation occurs. There is no reliable runtime detection of the IOCTL abuse once the driver is loaded. Prevention (HVCI, driver blocklist) is the only durable control.

ir-hunts.title: After-hours SharePoint bulk download from accounts outside peer group baseline: potential pre-exfiltration data staging
ir-hunts.premise: Defender for Cloud Apps UEBA generated two 'Mass Download' policy matches in the trailing 30 days, both automatically suppressed by an overly broad suppression rule. Manual review of SharePoint audit logs for the same window reveals three additional after-hours download clusters totaling approximately 8 GB from accounts outside their documented peer group norms, all between 01:00–04:00 UTC. This pattern is consistent with pre-exfiltration data staging; an attacker with compromised cloud credentials manually collecting research before moving it out. It is also consistent with legitimate after-hours remote work. The hypothesis cannot be confirmed or denied without data owner interviews and endpoint corroboration.
ir-hunts.huntScope: SharePoint Online and OneDrive audit logs (FileAccessed, FileDownloaded, FileSyncDownloadedFull events) for all accounts in the last 30 days. Filter to 20:00–06:00 UTC. Cross-reference anomalous accounts against Entra ID sign-in location history, endpoint Falcon telemetry, and manager interviews.
ir-hunts.dataSourcesNeeded:
SharePoint Online Unified Audit Log (M365 Compliance Center or via Sentinel OfficeActivity table)
Entra ID SigninLogs; source IP and location for accounts identified as anomalous
CrowdStrike Falcon; confirm endpoint activity during download windows for identified accounts
Manager/owner interviews; confirm or deny legitimacy of flagged download sessions
ir-hunts.dataSourcesAvailable:
Sentinel OfficeActivity connector (SharePoint UAL forwarded)
Entra ID SigninLogs (forwarded to Sentinel)
Falcon; available for all enrolled endpoints
Interviews; pending scheduling
ir-hunts.conclusionStatement:
INCONCLUSIVE; one account unresolved, hunt remains open.

Three download clusters identified:

1. svc-backup@corp.local; 3.1 GB from /sites/Research/ at 02:00 UTC nightly. Confirmed legitimate: documented automated backup process. Excluded from scope.

2. alex.hirsch@corp.local; 2.7 GB from /sites/NeuroResearch/ over 2 nights. Confirmed legitimate per manager interview: large document export for external conference presentation, performed from home IP consistent with Entra ID location history. Falcon shows no suspicious activity on host:HIRSCH-WKS-01 during those windows. Closed.

3. terry.ingram@corp.local; 4.1 GB from /sites/DARPA-Contract/ between 02:00–02:45 UTC on three consecutive nights (June 15, 16, 17). Entra ID sign-in source IP is consistent with user's home address. No IOCs on host:INGRAM-WKS-01 per Falcon. Manager was unaware of overnight work. terry.ingram has not yet responded to interview request.

Hunt remains OPEN on account 3. If interview produces no satisfactory explanation for the download pattern, escalate to formal investigation; the DARPA-Contract SharePoint library contains CUI-designated materials with mandatory breach reporting obligations.

techniques.name: Sharepoint
techniques.expectedIocs:
FileDownloaded or FileSyncDownloadedFull events exceeding 1 GB per session for standard user accounts
Download sessions from accounts occurring exclusively 20:00–06:00 UTC with no corresponding daytime activity
Downloads from SharePoint libraries tagged as sensitive (DARPA-Contract, NeuroResearch, IP-Vault) by accounts outside the documented owner group
techniques.notes: SharePoint UAL is available in M365 Compliance Center with 90-day retention (E3) or 1-year (E5). The OfficeActivity table in Sentinel has the same data if the connector is active. Key fields: Operation (FileDownloaded, FileSyncDownloadedFull), UserId, SiteUrl, SourceFileName, UserAgent. ClientIP anomaly versus Entra ID usual location is the highest-value correlation.
techniques.queryIdeas:
// Sentinel KQL; after-hours SharePoint bulk download hunt
OfficeActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where RecordType == "SharePointFileOperation"
| where Operation in ("FileDownloaded", "FileSyncDownloadedFull", "FileAccessed")
| where hourofday(TimeGenerated) between (20 .. 23) or hourofday(TimeGenerated) between (0 .. 6)
| summarize
    download_count = count(),
    sites = make_set(SiteUrl),
    files = make_set(SourceFileName)
    by UserId, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| where download_count > 100
| sort by download_count desc

// Peer group anomaly; compare to 30d personal baseline
OfficeActivity
| where RecordType == "SharePointFileOperation"
| where Operation == "FileDownloaded"
| summarize
    after_hours = countif(hourofday(TimeGenerated) < 6 or hourofday(TimeGenerated) > 20),
    total = count()
    by UserId
| extend after_hours_pct = round(100.0 * after_hours / total, 1)
| where after_hours_pct > 80 and total > 50
| sort by after_hours_pct desc

techniques.name: Data from Cloud Storage
techniques.expectedIocs:
Bulk API calls to SharePoint /drive/root/children or /me/drive for content enumeration preceding a download cluster
Microsoft Graph API calls from an IP inconsistent with the account's Entra ID usual sign-in location
techniques.notes: Attacker-controlled OAuth apps (like NeuraSyncPro in a compromised tenant) access SharePoint via Graph API rather than the browser client; these show a distinct UserAgent (Microsoft Graph API requests vs. OneDrive sync client). Filter on UserAgent to distinguish browser/sync-client downloads from programmatic API access.
techniques.queryIdeas:
// Distinguish browser downloads vs. Graph API access in UAL
OfficeActivity
| where RecordType == "SharePointFileOperation"
| where Operation == "FileDownloaded"
| extend ua = tostring(parse_json(AdditionalInfo).UserAgent)
| where ua contains "python-requests" or ua contains "PostmanRuntime" or ua contains "Go-http-client"
   or (ua !contains "OneDrive" and ua !contains "Mozilla" and ua !contains "Microsoft Office")
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, ClientIP, SourceFileName, ua, SiteUrl

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
techniques.expectedIocs:
Sustained high-volume egress to non-Microsoft IPs following a SharePoint download cluster (firewall/proxy NetFlow)
Direct-IP HTTPS (no SNI/DNS resolution) to non-SNI IP ranges shortly after large SharePoint file access events
techniques.notes: The download-then-exfiltrate pattern typically shows two distinct phases in proxy logs: first, a SharePoint/OneDrive download cluster (cloud-to-endpoint), then later, an endpoint-to-external upload (endpoint egress). Correlate the SharePoint UAL timestamps with NetFlow egress anomalies on the same host in the following 24–48 hours.
